title: Transition from unit squares to area formula
description: Find area of rectangles by multiplying side-lengths.
topic_path: Topic B: Concepts of area measurement
Transition from unit squares to area formula

assessment_items.question_content:
![a square.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bc67f4f0e4d41e9098b84a93e5fcfbef2cda7d8e) is $1$ unit square.

![a rectangle 6 squares wide and 4 squares high.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d59fdb0f5d4e3c3726353daa2b8a022394122f70)

**Which expressions can we use to find the area of the rectangle?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The area is the number of unit squares it takes to cover the rectangle.

![Same rectangle with width labeled 6 and height labeled 4.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/dd725c5d0c64f9a592fef89272931457e2ac22a5)

The rectangle has $\tealE{4}$ rows with $\goldE{6}$ unit squares per row.

###Repeated addition

We can find the area by adding the unit squares from each row:

$\underbrace{\goldE{6}+\goldE{6}+\goldE{6}+\goldE{6}}_{\tealE{4}\text{ rows}}$

###Multiplication

We can also write repeated addition as multiplication:

$\goldE{6}+\goldE{6}+\goldE{6}+\goldE{6}= \goldE{6} \times \tealE{4}$

We multiply $\goldE{\text{width}} \times \tealE{\text{height}}$.

We can use these expressions to find the area of the rectangle:

- $6+6+6+6$
- $6 \times 4$

assessment_items.question_content:
![a square.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a9d343d7f751c448f22a8129dc2139656049e07c) is $1$ unit square.

![a rectangle 3 squares wide and 9 squares high.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bfea6e75590f47d17bfb66bc52352b7dc7bbe2b4)

**Which expressions can we use to find the area of the rectangle?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The area is the number of unit squares it takes to cover the rectangle.

![Same rectangle with width labeled 3 and height labeled 9.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/84fe296cdb8b4518e889388058684ff657454f8e)

The rectangle has $\tealE{9}$ rows with $\goldE{3}$ unit squares per row.

###Repeated addition

We can find the area by adding the unit squares from each column:

$\underbrace{\tealE{9}+\tealE{9}+\tealE{9}}_{\goldE{3}\text{ columns}}$

###Multiplication

We can also write repeated addition as multiplication:

$\tealE{9}+\tealE{9}+\tealE{9}=\goldE{3} \times \tealE{9}$

We multiply $\goldE{\text{width}} \times \tealE{\text{height}}$.

We can use these expressions to find the area of the rectangle:

- $9+9+9$
- $3 \times 9$

###Repeated addition

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which expression shows a way to find the area of the following rectangle?**

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's imagine we finish drawing the unit squares in the rectangle.

- How many rows of unit squares will there be? 

- How many unit squares will be in each row?

[[☃ image 1]]

The rectangle has $\blueE{3}$ rows with $\maroonD{7}$ unit squares per row.
We can find the total number of unit squares by multiplying $\blueE{3} \times \maroonD{7}$ or $\maroonD{7} \times \blueE{3}$.
The expression $\maroonD{7} \times \blueE{3}$ shows a way to find the area of the rectangle.

options.alt: A rectangle divided by a partially complete grid of unit squares with 3 rows and 7 columns. Part of the rectangle is divided into 8 equal-sized squares. The remainder of the rectangle shows partially drawn rows and columns.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which equation shows a way to find the area of the following rectangle?**

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's imagine we finish drawing the unit squares in the rectangle.

- How many rows of unit squares will there be? 

- How many unit squares will be in each row?

[[☃ image 1]]

The rectangle has $\blueE{8}$ rows with $\maroonD{6}$ unit squares per row.
We can find the total number of unit squares by multiplying $\blueE{8} \times \maroonD{6}$ or $\maroonD{6} \times \blueE{8}$.
The equation $\maroonD{6}$ units${}\times \blueE{8}$ units $= 48$ square units shows a way to find the area of the rectangle.

options.alt: A rectangle divided by a partially complete grid of unit squares with 8 rows and 6 columns. Part of the rectangle is divided into 8 equal-sized squares. The remainder of the rectangle shows partially drawn rows and columns.

We can find the total number of unit squares by multiplying $\blueE{8} \times \maroonD{5}$ or $\maroonD{5} \times \blueE{8}$.
The expression $\blueE{8} \times \maroonD{5} $ shows a way to find the area of the rectangle.

options.alt: A rectangle divided by a partially complete grid of unit squares with 8 rows and 5 columns. Part of the rectangle is divided into 6 equal-sized squares. The remainder of the rectangle shows partially drawn rows and columns.

assessment_items.question_content:

We can find the total number of unit squares by multiplying $\blueE{4} \times \maroonD{9}$ or $\maroonD{9} \times \blueE{4}$.
The equation $\maroonD{9}$ units${}\times \blueE{4}$ units $=36$ square units shows a way to find the area of the rectangle.
